text: Software Engineering at Google defines software engineering as programming integrated over time. The key insight is that code will be modified, extended, and maintained by many people over many years — engineering practices exist to manage this long-term complexity, not just to ship features today.

Google Engineering — Sustainability: a codebase is sustainable if you can safely change everything that needs changing over its lifetime. Unsustainable codebases accumulate technical debt until changes become so risky and expensive that teams stop making them. Sustainability is an engineering responsibility, not a management decision.

Google Engineering — Code Review culture: every change is reviewed by at least one other engineer. Code review serves multiple purposes beyond catching bugs: knowledge sharing, maintaining consistency, collective code ownership, and organizational learning. Reviews should be fast — slow review cycles discourage small incremental changes and encourage large risky ones.

Google Engineering — Testing culture: Google distinguishes small tests (unit, no I/O, milliseconds), medium tests (integration, limited I/O, seconds), and large tests (E2E, full stack, minutes). Engineers are responsible for writing tests for their own code — testing is not a separate team's job. A feature is not done until it has tests.

Google Engineering — Toil: toil is repetitive, automatable, tactical work that scales linearly with service growth. Site Reliability Engineers (SRE) cap toil at 50% of work time — the rest goes to engineering that reduces future toil. If an operational task is done manually more than twice, automate it. Toil reduction is an investment in engineering capacity.

Google Engineering — Deprecation: removing old systems is as important as building new ones. Dead code and deprecated systems accumulate maintenance cost invisibly. Deprecation requires explicit ownership, migration paths, and deadlines. A system with no clear owner will never be deprecated — assign ownership before deprecation can happen.

Google Engineering — Large Scale Changes (LSC): changes affecting thousands of files across the codebase require tooling, not manual editing. Google built tools to make atomic large-scale refactoring possible. The lesson: if a change is painful to make once, it will never be made consistently at scale. Invest in tooling for changes you need to make repeatedly.
